does: This paper proves certified unlearning guarantees for stochastic versions of Descent-to-Delete (D2D) and Rewind-to-Delete (R2D) algorithms across strongly convex, convex, and nonconvex loss functions. The core contribution is establishing (ε, δ) differential privacy-style unlearning certificates for these gradient-based methods by analyzing unlearning as biased or disturbed gradient systems. The authors employ optimal coupling of random trajectories between unlearning and retraining to derive sensitivity bounds in expectation, comparing the theoretical guarantees between D2D and R2D across different loss landscapes. The results fill a critical gap: stochastic D2D (widely used as a finetuning baseline in practice) previously lacked theoretical justification for nonconvex settings.
connects: This work extends prior research on machine unlearning algorithms that aim to efficiently remove training data influence without full retraining. It builds directly on D2D and R2D, which were introduced as full-batch gradient descent variants with provable guarantees. The paper addresses the disconnect between theory and practice: while stochastic variants of these algorithms are commonly deployed in practice as finetuning baselines, they lacked formal guarantees beyond convex settings. The analysis connects to broader literature on differential privacy and certified data deletion, drawing techniques from coupled trajectory analysis and gradient flow theory to handle the stochastic, nonconvex regime where most modern neural networks operate.
enables: This theoretical framework enables practitioners to confidently deploy stochastic unlearning baselines with formal guarantees, supporting practical implementation of right-to-be-forgotten regulations. The coupling-based proof technique for analyzing perturbed gradient systems may extend to other stochastic optimization problems requiring sensitivity analysis. The work opens directions for tighter bounds on unlearning in nonconvex settings, potentially leveraging modern optimization theory. It could motivate development of unlearning algorithms specifically designed for the nonconvex regime with improved sample complexity, and provides foundations for understanding tradeoffs between computational efficiency and privacy guarantees in machine unlearning. The results may also apply to related problems like federated unlearning or continual learning with data removal.
